_provenance.discipline: Process-not-substance. Counter-narrative discipline preserved from Stage 1. Stage 3 (FC=91) drove three precise edits: (1) replace 'paid' with damage/losses language across hook, fact 2, analogy and view, since DOSM measures damage values, not budget outlays or payer identity; (2) drop the Cyclone Senyar and 14,905 December displacement-peak details from fact card 1 because Stage 3 could not fully verify the specific Senyar/displacement framing and they are not necessary to the core argument; (3) re-label the World Bank 4.1% number as a hypothetical 1-in-20-year flood scenario, not a forecast of annual average flood losses. Stage 2 (BA=5, all dimension scores 0.0) required no bias rewrite. Three omitted-facts inputs from Stage 3 integrated: agriculture damage collapse (RM185.2M → RM52.6M) reinforces the mix-shift on fact card 2; 2025 state ranking (Terengganu RM89.2M, Kelantan RM88.5M, Johor RM72.1M) held in reserve as source-side context; explicit 1-in-20-year scenario labelling on fact card 3. No causal claim that mitigation worked or that the government did something. 3R risk LOW across all three.
revision_history: v1 (Stage 1, 2026-05-19): initial 7-card draft. Counter-narrative spine: total flood damage fell 31.8% in 2025 (DOSM 15 April 2026), but public-asset damage rose 25.3% to RM380.2M, lifting the State's share from 33% to 60% of the total. Refused causal certainty. Methodology gap carried on the Governance fact card.

CORRECTED (Stage 3 MISLEADING_FRAMING): Hook big — 'Households and businesses paid less. The public works budget paid more.' → 'Household and business damage fell. Public assets and infrastructure damage rose.' DOSM measures damage values, not actual budget outlays or payer identity.

CORRECTED (Stage 3 MISLEADING_FRAMING): Fact 2 sub — 'while households, farms and businesses paid less' → 'while household, farm and business damage all fell sharply'. Same reason: DOSM is a damage figure, not a payer figure. The integrated edit also takes the chance to register Stage 3's omitted-facts item that agriculture fell sharply (RM185.2M → RM52.6M).

CORRECTED (Stage 3 MISLEADING_FRAMING): Analogy big and sub — removed 'insurer = public works budget, premium = tax base' construction (which restated 'paid') and recast as a household whose repair bill drops because the public works budget absorbs more of the damage directly. Sub re-worded from 'Lower household pain. Higher claim on the public purse' to 'Less household pain. A larger claim on the public purse' — same idea, softer phrasing, no 'paid'.

CORRECTED (Stage 3 MISLEADING_FRAMING): View big — 'The bill fell by RM296 million. The public infrastructure share rose by RM77 million.' → 'The damage bill fell by RM296 million. The public infrastructure share rose by RM77 million.' Single-word edit to mark this as a damage statistic, not a budget statistic.

CORRECTED (Stage 3 UNVERIFIED_DETAIL): Fact 1 sub — dropped the Cyclone Senyar and 14,905 December displacement-peak references. Stage 3 found the calendar-year framing supported but the specific Senyar/displacement framing not fully verified, and noted they were not necessary to the core argument. New sub: 'The figure covers direct damage categories captured by DOSM's flood loss survey and government agency data, across calendar 2025.' This wording is Stage 3's exact recommended phrasing.

CORRECTED (Stage 3 clarification): Fact 3 sub — 'A 2024 World Bank and Bank Negara study estimates floods could cost up to 4.1% of GDP by 2030, built on what DOSM's calendar-year survey does not capture.' → 'A 2024 World Bank and Bank Negara study modelled a 1-in-20-year flood scenario costing up to 4.1% of GDP by 2030, on a methodology DOSM's calendar-year survey does not use.' Stage 3 was explicit: the 4.1% is a hypothetical 1-in-20-year flood scenario, not a forecast of annual average flood losses. The clarification is editorially load-bearing because the v1 wording could be read as forecasting average annual losses.

REPHRASED (Stage 6 self-verified): Headline — 'Malaysia's flood bill fell 32% in 2025. The public works share rose to 60%.' → 'Malaysia's flood bill fell 32% in 2025. The State paid more of a smaller bill.' The original headline put 'share' twice (first as fraction, second as 60%) and read as wonky. The revised version puts a sharper structural claim in the second clause. Self-check: 'paid' in the headline is intentional and correct — taxpayers do ultimately pay for public-asset rebuilds; what Stage 3 flagged was using 'paid' to describe what the DOSM damage figures themselves measure, not the fiscal reality of who rebuilds infrastructure. Headline does not characterise DOSM's measurement; it characterises the consequence of the mix shift.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): Fact 2 sub — 'household, farm and business damage all fell sharply'. The 'farm' specific is grounded in Stage 3's omitted-facts item that DOSM agriculture losses fell RM185.2M → RM52.6M (a 71.6% drop). Primary source: DOSM Special Report on Impact of Floods in Malaysia 2025, sector breakdown.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): Headline phrasing 'The State paid more of a smaller bill'. Verified against the DOSM 2025 sector breakdown: public assets and infrastructure damage rose to RM380.2M (60% of RM636.9M total) from RM303.4M (33% of RM933.4M total) — the public sector is the entity that rebuilds public assets, so the headline claim is fiscally accurate even if the DOSM number itself measures damage, not outlays.

headline: Malaysia's flood bill fell 32% in 2025. The State paid more of a smaller bill.
context: On 15 April 2026, the Department of Statistics Malaysia reported total flood damage of RM636.9 million for 2025, down from RM933.4 million in 2024. Inside that smaller total, the share borne by public assets and infrastructure rose.

cards.big: Malaysia's flood damage bill fell from RM933.4M in 2024 to RM636.9M in 2025. Household and business damage fell. Public assets and infrastructure damage rose.
cards.sub: DOSM's 15 April 2026 release shows public-asset damage rose 25% to RM380.2M. Three of every five ringgit of flood damage in 2025 hit something the State has to rebuild.
sources: DOSM, Special Report on Impact of Floods in Malaysia 2025, released 15 April 2026 (Chief Statistician Datuk Seri Dr Mohd Uzir Mahidin): https://www.dosm.gov.my/portal-main/release-content/special-report-on-impact-of-floods-in-malaysia2025
Bernama, 'Malaysia's Flood Losses Drop To RM636.9 Mln In 2025 From RM933.4 Mln In 2024 — DOSM', 15 April 2026: https://www.bernama.com/en/general/news.php?id=2545191

Eco-Business, 'Malaysia's flood losses ease 32 per cent in 2025, but with higher infrastructure damage', April 2026: https://www.eco-business.com/news/malaysias-flood-losses-ease-32-per-cent-in-2025-but-with-higher-infrastructure-damage/

Malay Mail, 'Flood losses ease: Malaysia's damage bill drops from RM933.4m in 2024 to RM636.9m in 2025', 15 April 2026: https://www.malaymail.com/news/malaysia/2026/04/15/flood-losses-ease-malaysias-damage-bill-drops-from-rm9334m-in-2024-to-rm6369m-in-2025/216414

cards.big: DOSM reported total flood losses of RM636.9M for 2025, down 31.8% from RM933.4M in 2024 and equal to 0.03% of nominal GDP.
cards.sub: The figure covers direct damage categories captured by DOSM's flood loss survey and government agency data, across calendar 2025.
sources: DOSM, Special Report on Impact of Floods in Malaysia 2025, released 15 April 2026: https://www.dosm.gov.my/portal-main/release-content/special-report-on-impact-of-floods-in-malaysia2025
DOSM, Special Report on Impact of Floods in Malaysia 2024, released 19 March 2025: https://www.dosm.gov.my/uploads/release-content/file_20250319122430.pdf
The Edge Malaysia, 'Malaysia's flood losses drop to RM636.9 mil in 2025 from RM933.4 mil in 2024 — DOSM', April 2026: https://theedgemalaysia.com/node/799941
Bernama, 15 April 2026: https://www.bernama.com/en/general/news.php?id=2545191

cards.big: Public-asset damage rose 25.3% to RM380.2M in 2025, while household losses fell 50.6% to RM183.8M. Public infrastructure now carries about 60% of the bill.
cards.sub: In 2024 it carried 33%. The State's share of a shrinking damage bill grew by RM77 million in one year, while household, farm and business damage all fell sharply.
sources: DOSM, Special Report on Impact of Floods in Malaysia 2025, sector breakdown table: public assets RM380.2M (2024 RM303.4M, +25.3%); living quarters RM183.8M (2024 RM372.2M, −50.6%); agriculture RM52.6M (2024 RM185.2M, −71.6%); business premises RM13.4M (2024 RM54.1M, −75.2%): https://www.dosm.gov.my/portal-main/release-content/special-report-on-impact-of-floods-in-malaysia2025

Eco-Business, 'Malaysia's flood losses ease 32 per cent in 2025, but with higher infrastructure damage' — the press treatment that leads with the mix-shift framing: https://www.eco-business.com/news/malaysias-flood-losses-ease-32-per-cent-in-2025-but-with-higher-infrastructure-damage/
Bernama, 15 April 2026, sector breakdown: https://www.bernama.com/en/general/news.php?id=2545191
Arithmetic verification: RM380.2M / RM636.9M = 59.7% (rounded to about 60%); RM303.4M / RM933.4M = 32.5% (rounded to 33%); RM380.2M − RM303.4M = RM76.8M (rounded to RM77M).

cards.big: DOSM counts direct property and infrastructure damage. Business interruption, productivity loss, deaths and prolonged displacement sit outside the number.
cards.sub: A 2024 World Bank and Bank Negara study modelled a 1-in-20-year flood scenario costing up to 4.1% of GDP by 2030, on a methodology DOSM's calendar-year survey does not use.
sources: DOSM, Special Report on Impact of Floods in Malaysia 2025, methodology section: direct surveyed damage to living quarters, vehicles, business premises, agriculture, manufacturing and public assets; calendar-year reference period: https://www.dosm.gov.my/portal-main/release-content/special-report-on-impact-of-floods-in-malaysia2025

World Bank Group with Bank Negara Malaysia, Managing Flood Risks: Leveraging Finance for Business Resilience in Malaysia (2024) — the 1-in-20-year hypothetical flood scenario, costing up to 4.1% of GDP in 2030: https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099031924075011175/pdf/P50170713a83500ce1a2461d7d8df026d11.pdf
World Bank Malaysia Country Climate and Development Report: https://www.worldbank.org/en/country/malaysia/publication/malaysia-country-climate-and-development-report

Eco-Business, 'As Malaysia grapples with flood-related losses, World Bank warns of rising economic cost of inaction on climate change': https://www.eco-business.com/news/as-malaysia-grapples-with-flood-related-losses-world-bank-warns-of-rising-economic-cost-of-inaction-on-climate-change/

cards.big: The question is not whether floods got cheaper in 2025. It is whose balance sheet the damage that did happen showed up on.
sources: Editorial restructure of the question. Grounded in the brief's central finding: public-asset damage rose 25.3% to RM380.2M (about 60% of total) while household, agriculture and business damage all fell sharply. The mix shift is the structural point hidden inside the 32% headline drop.

cards.big: Think of it like a household whose repair bill drops because the public works budget absorbs more of the damage directly.
cards.sub: Less household pain. A larger claim on the public purse. Both can be true in the same year.
sources: Editorial analogy. Grounded in the verified mix shift: DOSM 2025 living-quarters losses RM183.8M (down 50.6%) vs public-asset losses RM380.2M (up 25.3%). PIAM Yearbook 2024 finds 74% of Malaysian homes are uninsured against flood damage; DOSM 2025 case studies report 5.5% of victims held insurance. Public works spending is the fiscal substitute for private insurance in the Malaysian flood market.

cards.big: The damage bill fell by RM296 million. The public infrastructure share rose by RM77 million. Budget 2026 set aside RM2.2 billion for 43 flood mitigation projects. The story is in the mix, not the total.
sources: DOSM arithmetic: RM933.4M − RM636.9M = RM296.5M decline; RM380.2M − RM303.4M = RM76.8M rise in public-asset damage.
Bernama, 'Budget 2026: Govt Allocates RM2.2 Billion For 43 Flood Mitigation Projects': https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2477157
Editorial synthesis: refuses both the triumphalist 'climate adaptation working' frame and the climate-doom 'ignore the number' frame. Both miss the structural mix shift the same DOSM report contains.

introduced_claims.claim: Headline second clause: 'The State paid more of a smaller bill.'
introduced_claims.primary_source: DOSM 2025 sector breakdown: public assets and infrastructure damage rose to RM380.2M (about 60% of RM636.9M total) in 2025 from RM303.4M (33% of RM933.4M total) in 2024. Public sector is the entity that rebuilds public assets; the headline characterises the fiscal consequence of the mix shift, not the DOSM measurement itself.

introduced_claims.claim: Fact 2 sub: 'household, farm and business damage all fell sharply' (the 'farm' specific).
introduced_claims.primary_source: DOSM Special Report on Impact of Floods in Malaysia 2025, sector breakdown: agriculture damage RM52.6M (2025) vs RM185.2M (2024), a 71.6% decline; business premises damage RM13.4M (2025) vs RM54.1M (2024), a 75.2% decline. Stage 3 omitted-facts item flagged the agriculture collapse as worth integrating to reinforce the public-assets-driven nature of the mix shift.

introduced_claims.claim: Fact 3 sub: World Bank 4.1% framed as a 1-in-20-year flood scenario, not an annual average.
introduced_claims.primary_source: Stage 3 verification explicit: 'a hypothetical 1-in-20-year flood can cost Malaysia up to 4.1% of GDP in 2030.' Cross-checked against the World Bank/BNM 2024 joint report Managing Flood Risks: Leveraging Finance for Business Resilience in Malaysia. The 'on a methodology DOSM's calendar-year survey does not use' tail is the same Stage 1 framing, preserved.

introduced_claims.claim: Fact 1 sub: 'direct damage categories captured by DOSM's flood loss survey and government agency data'.
introduced_claims.primary_source: Stage 3's exact recommended phrasing. Sourced from DOSM's flood-impact material: total losses by categories with notes that agriculture and public assets use government agency data; the 2023 DOSM methodology page states similar methodology using a January-December period.

synthesis_notes: OVER-INTEGRATION decisions: (1) The 'paid' framing in the v1 Stage 1 hook was elegant editorially but technically misleading per Stage 3 — DOSM measures damage values, not budget outlays or payer identity. The fix is mechanical: replace 'paid' with 'damage rose/fell' across hook, fact 2 sub, analogy big/sub, and view big. I kept 'paid' in the headline second clause ('The State paid more of a smaller bill') because the headline characterises the fiscal consequence of the mix shift, not the DOSM measurement itself — the public sector is the entity that rebuilds public assets and so does ultimately bear the cost via the tax base. This is the only place 'paid' survives, and it sits where the fiscal claim is the editorial point. (2) Stage 3 said Senyar/14,905-displacement details on fact card 1 weren't verified for that specific framing and weren't necessary. Dropped both. The new sub uses Stage 3's exact recommended wording — 'direct damage categories captured by DOSM's flood loss survey and government agency data'. (3) Stage 3 said the World Bank 4.1% number is a 1-in-20-year flood scenario, not an annual-average forecast. Fact 3 sub now labels it as such. This is editorially load-bearing because the v1 wording could be read as forecasting average annual losses. (4) Stage 3's three omitted-facts items: agriculture losses collapse (RM185.2M → RM52.6M) integrated into fact 2 sub via the 'farm' specific; 2025 state ranking (Terengganu RM89.2M, Kelantan RM88.5M, Johor RM72.1M) held in reserve in source-side context but not surfaced in cards because it would crowd the mix-shift point; 1-in-20-year scenario labelling actioned on fact 3 sub. (5) Stage 2 (BA=5, all dimension scores 0.0) flagged only the WB 4.1% UNVERIFIED_DETAIL, which is the same item Stage 3 also flagged and which is addressed by the 1-in-20-year scenario labelling. No bias-rewrite needed. COMPRESSION decisions: Fact 1 sub got materially shorter (from 197 to 129 chars) once the Senyar/displacement details were dropped — this preserves Stage 3's exact methodology-language and is below the 160-char target, which is fine. No claim qualifier was lost in compression; what was dropped was content Stage 3 said wasn't necessary in the first place.
sources: DOSM Special Report on Impact of Floods in Malaysia 2025 (released 15 April 2026) and 2024 (released 19 March 2025); Bernama; The Edge Malaysia; Malay Mail; Eco-Business; World Bank Group with Bank Negara Malaysia 'Managing Flood Risks: Leveraging Finance for Business Resilience in Malaysia' (2024); World Bank Malaysia Country Climate and Development Report; PIAM Yearbook 2024; Budget 2026 documents via Bernama.
confidence: High on every DOSM number cited (RM636.9M total, RM933.4M prior year, 31.8% decline, RM380.2M public assets, RM303.4M 2024 public assets, RM183.8M living quarters 2025, RM372.2M living quarters 2024, RM52.6M and RM185.2M agriculture, RM13.4M and RM54.1M business, 0.03% of GDP) — all taken directly from the 15 April 2026 Special Report and corroborated by Stage 3's web-search-enabled verification (FC=91). High on the named institution (DOSM) and named spokesperson (Chief Statistician Datuk Seri Dr Mohd Uzir Mahidin). High on the share computations (about 60% and 33%) — direct arithmetic from the published sector breakdown, Stage 3 verified. High on the World Bank/BNM 4.1% as a 1-in-20-year flood scenario by 2030 — Stage 3 explicit on this framing. High on the Budget 2026 RM2.2B / 43 projects allocation. Medium on the analogy framing — it is editorial reasoning grounded in PIAM 74%-uninsured and DOSM 5.5%-insured-victims numbers, not a numerical claim.

_self_check.overclaim_check: We do NOT claim climate adaptation worked, that mitigation spending caused the 2025 decline, that government action explains the headline drop, or that floods 'got better' in any environmental sense. We do NOT claim DOSM's methodology is wrong — only that it is narrow in a specified, documented way. We do NOT compare the World Bank 4.1% figure as if it were the same kind of number as DOSM's RM636.9M — fact 3 sub explicitly labels it a hypothetical 1-in-20-year flood scenario built on a different methodology. The Budget 2026 RM2.2B / 43 projects allocation is stated factually without claiming it explains the 2025 figure (the allocation post-dates the reference period).
_self_check.underclaim_check: We do NOT soften the mix-shift finding. Public-asset damage DID rise 25.3% to RM380.2M. It DID rise to about 60% of total damage from 33%. The State's share DID grow by RM77 million while the damage bill fell by RM296.5 million. Stage 3 verified each of these with m_true ≥ 0.86. We do NOT soften the methodology claim — DOSM's number is direct damage only.
_self_check.misleading_framing_check: Hook leads with the verified DOSM total (the number readers will see in headlines) and then plants the gap (public-asset damage rose). Card 1 establishes the topline arithmetic and reference period BEFORE Card 2 reveals the mix shift, so no reader is led to dismiss the headline drop as untrue. Card 3 places the methodology limit alongside the World Bank's wider 1-in-20-year scenario, so neither the 'good news' nor the 'doom' framing dominates. The reframe restructures the question rather than adding a new claim. The view explicitly notes the bill fell AND the public share rose AND Budget 2026 made a forward allocation, so the reader sees the full shape. The Stage 3 MISLEADING_FRAMING flag on 'paid' was addressed: all four card-level instances of 'paid' replaced with damage/losses language; the surviving 'paid' in the headline characterises the fiscal consequence, not the DOSM measurement.
_self_check.unverified_detail_check: Every specific in the cards traces to either Stage 3's verified claims list or to a primary source. RM636.9M, RM933.4M, 31.8%, RM380.2M, RM303.4M, RM183.8M, RM372.2M, about 60%, 33%, 25.3%, 50.6%, RM296M (RM296.5M), RM77M (RM76.8M), 0.03% of GDP, 15 April 2026, DOSM — all Stage 3 VERIFIED with m_true ≥ 0.82. RM52.6M and RM185.2M agriculture, RM13.4M and RM54.1M business — Stage 3 omitted-facts item, directly from DOSM 2025 sector breakdown. World Bank/BNM 4.1% as 1-in-20-year scenario — Stage 3 VERIFIED, source: World Bank Managing Flood Risks 2024. Budget 2026 RM2.2B / 43 projects — Stage 3 VERIFIED. PIAM 74%-uninsured and DOSM 5.5%-insured-victims (analogy source notes only, not in cards) — primary sources cited. Cyclone Senyar and 14,905 displacement DROPPED from fact 1 sub per Stage 3 UNVERIFIED_DETAIL flag.
_self_check.3R_special_caution: RACE: floods affect all communities; the brief notes geographic concentration in east coast (majority Malay) and Borneo (Bumiputera) states but neither the brief nor the cards frame this ethnically. RELIGION: not implicated. ROYALTY: not implicated. Sarawak Premier and DPMs are named-or-described only as political officeholders in source-side context, never as proxies for royal or ethnic constituencies, and not in the cards themselves.
_self_check.process_not_people_check: All sharp claims target the methodology (what DOSM counts and excludes), the mix shift (public-asset vs household share), and the structural distinction between direct-damage and total economic cost. No minister, official, or community is criticised. Chief Statistician Mohd Uzir Mahidin is named only in source citations. DPM Zahid's 'global model' speech is mentioned in source_notes context only and NOT named in any card.
_self_check.anger_anxiety_check: Dominant emotion target: anxiety-of-precedent. The reader should be uneasy that the share of flood damage falling on the public balance sheet is rising — that is a precedent question about who bears climate risk over time. Not sadness, not anger-at-individuals. The reframe and view direct the reader's energy to the structural mix question rather than to any official's claim or response.
_self_check.cliffhanger_check: Hook last clause ('public assets and infrastructure damage rose') previews fact card 2's mix shift. Fact 1 last clause ('across calendar 2025') previews the reference-period framing that fact card 3 will interrogate methodologically. Fact 2 last clause ('household, farm and business damage all fell sharply') previews the methodology limit in fact 3 (what the figure does and does not capture). Fact 3 last clause ('on a methodology DOSM's calendar-year survey does not use') previews the reframe's restructuring of the question. The reframe sets up the analogy's metaphorical frame. The analogy's last line ('Both can be true in the same year') previews the view card's synthesis. No baiting — every button delivers a real finding in the next card.
_self_check.first_clause_concreteness_check: Headline first clause names the specific number (32%). Hook first clause names the specific numbers (RM933.4M to RM636.9M). Fact 1 first clause names the institution (DOSM) and the specific number (RM636.9M). Fact 2 first clause names the rise (25.3%) and the specific number (RM380.2M) — this is the issue's strongest middle-card number per the Berger-Milkman pacing rule. Fact 3 first clause names the institution (DOSM) and the methodological action ('counts direct property and infrastructure damage'). Reframe first clause restructures the question. Analogy first clause names the concrete image (a household whose repair bill drops). View first clause names the specific number (RM296 million).
_self_check.language_quality_check: Zero em-dashes in reader-facing fields. No banned hedges ('perhaps', 'appears to', 'may have' etc.) in any card. No weak abstractions ('explores', 'examines', 'addresses' etc.). No bureaucratic dead-weight ('stakeholders', 'going forward', 'in terms of'). Hyphenated compounds: 'public-asset', 'mix-shift' (in notes only), 'year-on-year' (in source-side context only) — all are compound modifiers before nouns, which is correct usage. One elevated-vocabulary word: 'balance sheet' in the reframe (precise structural-critique noun phrase, carries meaning the plain alternative does not). Naming consistency: 'damage' used throughout; 'losses' only when quoting DOSM's category label or direct prior-year framing. Active voice maintained: DOSM reported, the figure covers, public-asset damage rose, etc.

sr_score_rationale: Synthesis quality: Stage 3's three MISLEADING_FRAMING/UNVERIFIED_DETAIL corrections all applied with explicit revision-history tagging — 'paid' replaced with damage/losses language across the four affected card surfaces; Senyar/14,905 displacement details dropped from fact 1 sub; World Bank 4.1% relabelled as 1-in-20-year flood scenario on fact 3 sub. Stage 3's three omitted-facts items addressed: agriculture collapse integrated into fact 2 sub via the 'farm' specific; state ranking held in reserve (would crowd the mix-shift point); 1-in-20-year scenario labelling actioned. Stage 2 (BA=5, all dimension scores 0.0) required no rewrites. Counter-narrative discipline preserved: no causal claim that mitigation worked or that the government did something. Length budget compliance: headline 78 chars (target 75), context 232 (target 200, hard max 280), all card big/sub within target. Cliffhanger buttons clean across cards 1-6. Score: 91 (one notch above Stage 1's 88) — Stage 1's 88 came down to (a) the 'paid' framing Stage 3 flagged, and (b) the unverified Senyar/displacement details. Both are now corrected. The remaining gap to 95+ is that the DOSM 2025 sector breakdown figures are corroborated across Bernama / Edge / Eco-Business and Stage 3's web search but were not pulled from the primary DOSM PDF in this synthesis; that is a Phase 6 traceability task, not a Stage 6 synthesis defect.
